title: VI→III→i Loop — F Minor
description: VI→III→i in F minor — Dbmaj7 → Abmaj → Fm. You derive every chord name from the F minor scale using the count-from-root method, rather than memorising. This is the method that makes any key accessible.
what_youll_learn: Deriving VI→III→i chord names from F minor using semitone counting
How Dbmaj7 → Abmaj → Fm voicings differ from the Bb minor versions
The method (count from root) over the result (specific chord names)
How the same progression in a different key retains the same emotional function

steps.title: Derive the F minor chord names — don't look them up
steps.instruction: Before opening Ableton: work out the VI→III→i chord names in F minor. Root = F. VI = 9 semitones up from F (count: F→Gb→G→Ab→A→Bb→B→C→Db→D = 9). VI = Db major. III = 3 semitones up from F = Ab major. i = Fm. Write down: Dbmaj7 → Abmaj → Fm. Now open Ableton, BPM 140, tracks DRUMS/BASS/PAD.
steps.why: Working out the chord names before opening Ableton is the entire point. If you look them up first, you've skipped the method. The method is: root + semitone counting. That method works in any key. The specific chords are just the output.
steps.tip: Verify: Db major has 3 notes: Db, F, Ab. F minor has 3 notes: F, Ab, C. Abmaj has 3 notes: Ab, C, Eb. All of these notes are in the F natural minor scale (F, G, Ab, Bb, C, Db, Eb). You can verify any chord is in-key by checking its notes against the scale.

steps.title: Program drums
steps.instruction: Copy 4-on-floor drum pattern at 140 BPM from rep-11. Identical setup.
steps.why: Same tempo, same drums. The only thing changing from rep-11 is the key. This isolates the key variable.
steps.tip: Copy the whole drum track from rep-11 if possible.

steps.title: Program F minor bass roots
steps.instruction: Sub bass. 4-bar clip: bars 1–2 Db1 (VI root), bar 3 Ab0/Ab1 (III root), bar 4 F1 (i root). Hold each root for its full chord duration. This is the VI→III→i root movement in F minor.
steps.why: Same relative motion as rep-11 but at different pitches. Db1 is slightly higher than Gb1 — the Kettama rep. F1 is higher than Bb0. The progression feels similar in quality but sits at a different register.
steps.tip: Compare: Bb minor roots (Gb1→Db1→Bb0) vs F minor roots (Db1→Ab1→F1). The F minor roots are 7 semitones higher. At 140 BPM both feel equally driving.

steps.title: Voice the Dbmaj7 chord
steps.instruction: Serum chord pad on PAD. Dbmaj7 voicing: Db3, F3, Ab3, C4. Major 7th (C) on top. Spread across 1.5 octaves. Hold for 2 full bars.
steps.why: Dbmaj7 = Db, F, Ab, C. The voicing puts C4 on top (major 7th). This is the same structural decision as Gbmaj7 in rep-11 — 7th on top, spread voicing, nothing below C3.
steps.tip: Check: is Db3 your lowest pad note? Good. Is C4 your highest? Good. Spread: Db3 to C4 = approximately 1 octave. This is slightly narrower than Gbmaj7's spread but still a spread voicing.

steps.title: Voice Abmaj and Fm
steps.instruction: Bar 3: Abmaj — Ab3, C4, Eb4. Bar 4: Fm — F3, Ab3, C4. Notice Fm shares Ab3 and C4 with Abmaj — those two notes stay as the chord resolves to the tonic. Hold each for 1 bar.
steps.why: The common tones between Abmaj and Fm (Ab and C) make the final chord change smooth. This is the same common tone principle as in rep-11 — the progression moves but something familiar remains in place.
steps.tip: Listen to bar 3 → bar 4 transition. Ab and C stay. F3 arrives as the new root below them on bar 4. The bass confirms F by moving to F1. Together they create an unambiguous tonic arrival.

steps.title: Apply sidechain + reverb (full setup from memory)
steps.instruction: Without referring to previous reps: set up kick sidechain on PAD (ratio 6:1, attack 0.5ms, release 300ms), reverb return (HP 200Hz, decay 4s, pre-delay 20ms), pad send at -10dB, EQ pad HP at 130Hz. This should take under 3 minutes.
steps.why: By rep-12 this is the standard garage pad setup. It should be muscle memory. If it takes more than 5 minutes you need to revisit reps 08–11.

images.caption: Compressor with Sidechain panel — 'Sidechain' triangle at top-right, power button to enable, 'Audio From' set to DRUMS. This is the full setup you should be able to do from memory by rep-12.

steps.tip: Time yourself: sidechain setup, reverb return creation, EQ. Target: under 3 minutes total.

steps.title: Compare F minor vs Bb minor side by side
steps.instruction: Export this loop. Play rep-11 (Bb minor) and rep-12 (F minor) back to back. Listen for: the emotional similarity (both use VI→III→i, both have the same pump and reverb), the timbral difference (Db/Ab/F vs Gb/Db/Bb — different specific pitches), the register difference (F minor sits slightly higher).
steps.why: This comparison confirms that you've transposed the technique correctly. Both loops should feel like they come from the same musical world — same progression, same production approach, different key. If one sounds fundamentally different, there's a voicing or timing error.
steps.tip: If they sound too similar (boring) rather than similar-but-distinct, you've got it right. The technique is the same — the key creates the distinction.

steps.title: Transpose the full Bb minor rep to F minor using Shift+Arrow
steps.instruction: Optional: copy all MIDI clips from rep-11. Select all pad notes (Cmd+A). Press Shift+Up arrow 7 times (7 semitones up = a perfect fifth = Bb to F). Compare this transposed version against your hand-built version from step 3–5. They should produce identical or near-identical results.
steps.why: This verifies your hand-built voicings are correct. If the Shift+Arrow transposition and your hand-built version produce the same chords, your count-from-root method was accurate. If they differ, work out which notes are correct and why.
steps.tip: 7 semitones = perfect fifth = Bb to F. This is a useful transposition to memorise: +7 semitones moves any pattern up a fifth.
